nativeRange.habitat: Large, slow-moving rivers, floodplain lakes, billabongs, and swamps in tropical northern Australia and southern New Guinea; calm or sluggish backwaters near the surface with overhanging vegetation

decorPreferences: large open swimming area at the surface
large smooth rocks or driftwood at edges for territory definition
dim to moderate lighting to reduce stress
surface cover of floating plants optional

diet.notes: In the wild, the Jardini Arowana is a surface ambush predator that feeds on fish, frogs, large insects, small birds, and other prey it can take at or near the water's surface. In captivity, newly acquired fish are generally started on large feeder fish, then gradually transitioned onto market prawns, whole fish such as smelt or whitebait, large earthworms, and high-quality carnivore sticks or pellets. The transition away from live prey can take time but is generally achievable with patience. Adults should be fed two to three times per week rather than daily to avoid obesity, while juveniles can be fed daily. Uneaten food should be removed promptly, as waste from a large piscivore degrades water quality quickly.

avoidWith: all other fish in most cases (piscivore — will eat any tankmate that fits in its mouth)
other arowanas (intense territorial aggression between conspecifics)
mid-water or surface fish of any size (ambush risk or harassment)

imageCandidates.notes: Lateral view of adult specimen showing characteristic pearlescent scale margins and barbels. Verify resolution before use.

summary: The Jardini Arowana, also known as the Northern Saratoga, is a large predatory fish from northern Australia and Papua New Guinea that can reach 90 cm in length. An obligate air-breather and aggressive surface piscivore, it is suited only to species-only tanks of 1500 L or more and is considered a specialist-level fish.
careNotes: The Jardini Arowana (Scleropages jardinii) is one of two Australian Saratoga species, distinguished from the Southern Saratoga (S. leichardti) by its more northerly range across Queensland’s Gulf Country, the Northern Territory, and southern Papua New Guinea, its larger adult size, and the pearlescent crescent markings that rim each scale. Like all members of the family Osteoglossidae (the bonytongues), it is an obligate air-breather — its modified swim bladder functions as a primitive lung, meaning it must have continuous, unobstructed access to the water’s surface at all times. Never block the airspace above the water in this species’ tank. Reaching up to 90 cm and displaying intense territorial aggression toward any perceived rival, the Jardini Arowana is realistically suitable only as a single specimen in a purpose-built tank of 1500 L or more with a footprint of at least 300 cm × 100 cm. A heavy, tight-fitting lid with no gaps is essential — this species is a powerful, athletic jumper that will launch itself out of any opening it finds. Water quality must be kept pristine: the heavy waste load produced by carnivore feeding demands robust biological filtration with high turnover, and large weekly water changes in the range of 30–50% are standard practice. Temperature should be maintained in the warm tropical range of 24–30°C; soft to moderately hard, near-neutral to slightly acidic water is preferred. Subdued lighting is generally recommended, as bright light can increase stress-related surface striking behavior. Australian export restrictions mean that wild-caught specimens are not legally exported from Australia; the aquarium trade is supplied by captive-bred stock from Australian and Papua New Guinean farms and licensed breeders. The Jardini Arowana is not subject to CITES Appendix I listing — unlike the Asian Arowana ([fw-fish-143]) — but Australian wildlife export law still applies to wild specimens. The common name ‘Northern Saratoga’ is widely used to distinguish this species from the ‘Southern Saratoga’ (S. leichardti), and the species epithet is spelled jardinii (with a double ‘i’), in honour of explorer F.L. Jardine.
breedingNotes: The Jardini Arowana is a paternal mouthbrooder, a reproductive strategy it shares with the Asian Arowana ([fw-fish-143]). After spawning, the male takes the large, yolk-rich eggs — up to 200 per clutch — into his mouth and incubates them orally for approximately six to seven weeks, continuing to brood the free-swimming fry after hatching until the yolk sac is fully absorbed. Captive breeding is extremely rare, owing to the immense space a compatible pair requires and the intense aggression both sexes can display toward one another. A minimum 3000 L purpose-built breeding pond or enclosure is generally advised for any serious attempt. Distinguishing the sexes externally is difficult; females are generally larger and noticeably deeper-bodied when gravid. Successful captive breeding has been reported in large specialist facilities and public aquaria. Fry are large at the time of release and are typically able to take whole small fish and earthworms within days.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Scleropages jardinii
primary.notes: Primary source for taxonomy, water parameters, native range, adult size, diet, temperament, and aquarium requirements. Confirms paternal mouthbrooding, surface air-breathing, 90 cm adult size, and species-only recommendation.

additional.name: FishBase — Scleropages jardinii
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 90 cm TL), native range (Queensland, Northern Territory, southern New Guinea), taxonomy (Osteoglossidae, Osteoglossiformes), temperature and pH ranges, diet (carnivore/piscivore), IUCN status, and depth preference.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Arowana Care Guide
additional.notes: Supplementary trade/hobby guidance on arowana care applicable to jardinii: tank size requirements, lid security for jumping behaviour, feeding transition from live prey to prepared foods, and water quality management for large carnivores.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Arowana Species Guide
additional.notes: Hobbyist-focused coverage of Scleropages species including jardinii: distinguishes Northern Saratoga (S. jardinii) from Southern Saratoga (S. leichardti), covers Australian export regulations, confirms paternal mouthbrooding behaviour, and discusses captive breeding rarity.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Saratoga: Australia’s Native Arowana
additional.notes: Species account covering biology of both Australian Saratoga species; confirms jardinii distribution in northern Australia and PNG, obligate air-breathing, aggressive piscivore behaviour, and specialist-only suitability for home aquaria.

fish.breedingNotes: The male incubates up to approximately 200 large eggs orally for six to seven weeks, then continues brooding free-swimming fry until the yolk sac is absorbed. Captive breeding is exceptionally rare outside large specialist facilities and requires enormous enclosures and a compatible, non-lethal pairing.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Reaches 90cm; aggressive piscivore needing 1500L+ species-only tank. Air-breather. Australia/PNG endemic; not as legally restricted as Asian Arowana (S. formosus) but Australian export restrictions apply.
